In the ancient world, a new king might kill members of the former royal family to remove rivals to the throne. Saul later made a similar request of David (1 Samuel 24:21).

title: 1 Samuel 20:15
content_html: cut off your loving devotion from my household: David later cared for Jonathan’s son, Mephibosheth, like his own son (2 Samuel 9:1–13).

title: 1 Samuel 20:17
content_html: See 1 Samuel 18:3 and 23:16–18.

title: 1 Samuel 20:18–22
content_html: Jonathan's plan would allow David to get the message even if there could be no face-to-face meeting. The boy's presence reduced suspicion and gave Jonathan an excuse to go to the field. As it happened, only the boy went with Jonathan to the field. When the boy left, David could talk to Jonathan directly (1 Samuel 20:40–42).

title: 1 Samuel 20:25
content_html: Abner was Saul’s cousin and served as commander of Saul’s army (1 Samuel 14:50; 17:55). His importance is shown by his position beside the king at this festival.

title: 1 Samuel 20:26–27
content_html:
• A number of circumstances could render a person ceremonially unclean and thus unfit for attending a sacred festival (see Leviticus 7:2–21). Often, an unclean person was required to be excluded from such functions only until the evening of the same day (Leviticus 15). This explains why Saul became suspicious after David did not arrive the next day.

  • Saul referred to David as the son of Jesse (also in 1 Samuel 20:31; 22:7), a description that functioned as David’s last name.

title: 1 Samuel 20:29
content_html: let me go: This verb also describes how David escaped from Saul earlier (1 Samuel 19:10–12, 17–18).

title: 1 Samuel 20:30
content_html:
• You son of a perverse and rebellious woman!: This insult was as strong and offensive in Hebrew as it is in English.

  • to your own shame: Saul cared about his family's legacy. He saw David’s actions as a threat to Jonathan, the rightful heir. To Saul, Jonathan was stupidly hurting and shaming himself by supporting David.

title: 1 Samuel 20:33
content_html:
• Saul hurled his spear at Jonathan: Saul had tried to kill others in this same way before (see also 1 Samuel 18:11; 19:10).

  • so Jonathan knew: Jonathan had been slow to accept the truth about his own father (1 Samuel 20:2–3). Now he could no longer deny it.

title: 1 Samuel 20:41
content_html:
• David … bowed: Jonathan had a higher social rank, making David's gesture appropriate.

  • he and Jonathan … wept: For the first time in the story, the text shows that both men openly shared their grief (see 1 Samuel 18:1, 3–4; 20:17).

title: 1 Samuel 20:42
content_html: sworn in the name of the LORD: (See 1 Samuel 18:3; 20:15–17; 23:16–18).

title: 1 Samuel 21:1
content_html:
• Nob was just north of Jerusalem, in southern Benjamin's territory.

  • Ahimelech the priest was a descendant of the condemned family of Eli (1 Samuel 14:3; 22:9).

  • The elders of Bethlehem were afraid when Samuel suddenly came to their city to anoint David as Saul’s replacement (1 Samuel 16:4). A similar fear appears here. In both situations, a well-known person who had problems with Saul arrived without warning. Both Ahimelech and the elders of Bethlehem feared that Saul might accuse them of helping an enemy of the king. Such suspicion could lead to death. In this case, what they feared did happen (see 1 Samuel 22:9–19).

title: 1 Samuel 21:2
content_html: “The king has given me a mission,”: David lied to hide that he was an outlaw and to ease Ahimelech's concerns.

title: 1 Samuel 21:4
content_html:
• Only priests could eat the holy bread (see Exodus 25:22–30; Leviticus 24:5–9), and only within sacred areas.

  • kept themselves from women: The holy bread was for those who were ceremonially pure. The priest checked that David’s men were not ceremonially impure from sexual activity (see Leviticus 15:16–18). Normally, people who were not priests could not eat this bread. The priest made an exception because he saw the men's needs and believed they were on a royal mission.

title: 1 Samuel 21:6
content_html:
• gave him the consecrated bread: Jesus referred to this event to show that helping people is more important than strict obedience to religious rules (see Matthew 12:1–8; Mark 2:23–28; Luke 6:1–5).

  • The Bread of the Presence included twelve loaves, possibly one for each tribe. The priests placed them on a table in the Holy Place. They were replaced every Sabbath (see Exodus 25:30; Leviticus 24:5–9).

title: 1 Samuel 21:7
content_html: detained before the LORD: Ahimelech asked God for guidance on behalf of others (1 Samuel 22:15). Because of this, Doeg may have been waiting for an answer to a question he had brought before God.

title: 1 Samuel 21:9
content_html:
• David used Goliath's sword to cut off the giant's head (1 Samuel 17:51). Both the sword and Goliath's head were taken as war trophies (17:54).

  • The ephod mentioned here might not be the priestly garment. Some passages suggest an ephod could also be a statue (Judges 8:27; 18:17).

title: 1 Samuel 21:10–15
content_html: Gath, a major Philistine city and the hometown of Goliath, was a clever but dangerous place for David to seek refuge from Saul.

title: 1 Samuel 21:11
content_html: king of the land: David’s reputation as a powerful warrior had become even greater than King Saul’s (see 1 Samuel 18:7). Because of this, people outside Israel began to speak of David as if he were already Israel’s king.

title: 1 Samuel 21:12–13
content_html: The long title of Psalm 34 mentions this event but calls the Philistine king Abimelech instead of Achish. "Abimelech" might have been a general title for Philistine kings (in Hebrew, it means "my father the king"). The name Abimelech also appears in Genesis 20:2 and 26:1.

title: 1 Samuel 22:1
content_html:
• Adullam was about 25.8 kilometers (16 miles) southwest of Jerusalem, near Philistine land.

  • If David's brothers and other relatives had stayed in Bethlehem, Saul could have easily targeted them for revenge.

title: 1 Samuel 22:1–23
content_html: Saul’s character is clear here. He could not take revenge on David himself. Instead, Saul killed those he believed had helped David.

title: 1 Samuel 22:2
content_html: distressed … indebted … discontented: The people who gathered around David were distressed, poor, and unhappy. By drawing such people to himself, David pointed ahead to Jesus, who would later welcome people who were hurting, burdened, and pushed to the edges of society (Matthew 11:25–30).

title: 1 Samuel 22:3–4
content_html:
• Mizpeh in Moab was east of the Dead Sea and the Jordan River. It was a difficult journey from Adullam, especially if David's 400 men traveled with him (1 Samuel 22:2).

  • they stayed with him: Jesse's grandmother, Ruth, was a Moabite (Ruth 4:13–22).

title: 1 Samuel 22:5
content_html: The prophet Gad was David’s special adviser (2 Samuel 24:11). God had stopped communicating with Saul and was now guiding David.

title: 1 Samuel 22:7–8
content_html: men of Benjamin: Saul was suspicious of his own tribesmen. He believed David had paid them to be loyal to him. Worse, Saul did not even trust his own son. He believed his son was part of the supposed conspiracy.

title: 1 Samuel 22:13
content_html: David had deceived Ahimelech into thinking he was on a mission from Saul (1 Samuel 21:1–9). Because of this, Saul’s accusations of conspiracy had no basis.

title: 1 Samuel 22:14
content_html: the king’s son-in-law: David had married Saul's daughter (1 Samuel 18:17–29).

title: 1 Samuel 22:18
content_html:
• Doeg the Edomite … struck down the priests: Doeg was not an Israelite, so he did not respect the holiness of priests chosen by God. He knew the priests were innocent, yet he still killed them. In this way, Doeg partly fulfilled God’s earlier judgment against the priestly family of Eli (see 1 Samuel 2:27–34 and 1 Kings 2:27).

  • men who wore the linen ephod: This incident shows how irrational Saul had become. Not even those in sacred office were safe.

title: 1 Samuel 22:19
content_html: Saul had been unwilling to carry out God’s orders to destroy completely the Amalekites and their possessions (1 Samuel 15:3, 9). Yet now, in his rage, he did not hesitate to destroy the priests' families and their possessions (see Proverbs 14:29; 16:14; 27:4).

title: 1 Samuel 22:20
content_html: Abiathar became an adviser to David, providing guidance from the Lord through the ephod (a priestly garment) (1 Samuel 23:2–12; 2 Samuel 15:24–36). Later, he conspired with Adonijah against David and Solomon (1 Kings 1–2).

title: 1 Samuel 23:1
content_html: Keilah was near Adullam (1 Samuel 22:1). It was at the western edge of Judah, near the Philistine border (see Joshua 15:44; Nehemiah 3:17–18).

title: 1 Samuel 23:1–29
content_html: Because Saul received reports from informants, he was able to continue pursuing David (1 Samuel 23:7,13). David also had informants, which helped him stay ahead of Saul (1 Samuel 23:1, 9, 15, 25). David’s greatest advantage was that he could seek guidance and help from God, which Saul did not have (1 Samuel 23:2, 4, 12).

title: 1 Samuel 23:4
content_html: David asked the Lord for guidance, either by praying directly or by using the ephod (a priestly garment used to seek God’s will), as described in 1 Samuel 23:6, 9; 2 Samuel 2:1; and 2 Samuel 5:23–24. God’s answers were a sign of his blessing and protection (compare 1 Samuel 28:6).

title: 1 Samuel 23:6
content_html: the ephod: Saul killed 85 priests who wore ephods (1 Samuel 22:18; see study note on 1 Samuel 2:18). However, the priest who escaped had the high priest's ephod, which held the Urim and Thummim (sacred objects used for decision-making).

title: 1 Samuel 23:7
content_html: God has delivered him into my hand: This statement is not true. David later explains that Saul’s claim is wrong (see 1 Samuel 23:14).

title: 1 Samuel 23:14
content_html:
• The wilderness strongholds were natural rock formations that offered shelter.

  • Ziph was about 16 kilometers (10 miles) southeast of Keilah (1 Samuel 23:1).

title: 1 Samuel 23:16
content_html: Jonathan encouraged David because he knew God had chosen David to be king.

title: 1 Samuel 23:17
content_html: you will be king … I will be your second-in-command (literally second to you): Jonathan accepted that God chose David as king and gave up his own ambitions to be king. Jonathan never held an official role in David’s court and died in battle with his father (1 Samuel 31:2).

title: 1 Samuel 23:18
content_html:
• a covenant: See 1 Samuel 18:3–4; 20:12–17.

  • Jonathan went home: This was likely the last time David and Jonathan saw each other.

title: 1 Samuel 23:19
content_html:
• Hakilah was a hill in the wilderness region of Ziph (1 Samuel 23:14).

  • Jeshimon is sometimes translated as “wasteland” rather than as a place name. Here, it refers to a specific wasteland north of the Dead Sea, on both banks of the Jordan River (see Numbers 21:20).

title: 1 Samuel 23:20
content_html: Keilah (1 Samuel 23:12) and Ziph (23:14; Psalm 54:1) were located in Judah, but neither one provided sanctuary for David, a native of Judah. David’s rise to power was not the result of his own tribe’s loyalty but because of the will of God.

title: 1 Samuel 23:24
content_html:
• Maon was about 4.8 kilometers (3 miles) directly south of the wilderness of Ziph (1 Samuel 23:14), about 16 kilometers (10 miles) south of Hebron.

  • The Arabah Valley is a deep rift stretching from above the Sea of Galilee down both sides of the Jordan River to the Dead Sea.

title: 1 Samuel 23:28
content_html: Sela-hammahlekoth: The Hebrew name is often translated "Rock of Escape." People in ancient Israel often gave place names to remember their thanks to God (for example, Genesis 22:14; 28:19; 32:30). The exact rock formation that received this name is unknown.

title: 1 Samuel 23:29
content_html: David traveled eastward from the area of Ziph (1 Samuel 23:14). He found safety in the strongholds of En-gedi. These rocky highlands were on the western shore of the Dead Sea. There was an oasis in this region, making it an ideal place to find water and food.

title: 1 Samuel 24:2
content_html: three thousand chosen men: David's force was outnumbered five to one (1 Samuel 23:13).

title: 1 Samuel 24:4
content_html:
• David’s men believed, as Saul did, that if the circumstances seem right, the Lord is telling you through those circumstances what to do (see 1 Samuel 23:7).

  • David might have cut a piece of Saul’s robe without getting caught because Saul either took off the robe and set it aside or fell asleep in the cave.

title: 1 Samuel 24:5
content_html: David felt guilty because he had dishonored God's anointed king.

title: 1 Samuel 24:6
content_html: The Lord's anointed one was not to be harmed (1 Samuel 26:9, 11, 16, 23; 2 Samuel 1:14, 16; 19:21). David refused to hurt Saul, even though God had chosen David to be the next king (1 Samuel 16:13).

title: 1 Samuel 24:11
content_html: my father: Saul was actually David’s father-in-law (1 Samuel 18:27). David was demonstrating his respect for the king and recalling a day when their relationship had been much friendlier.

title: 1 Samuel 24:12
content_html:
• May the LORD judge: There was no human authority who could decide what was right between Saul and David. Because of this, David asked God to judge the matter (see Genesis 16:5; 31:53; Exodus 5:21; Judges 11:27).

  • may the LORD take vengeance on you: David trusted God's plan instead of trying to control it.

title: 1 Samuel 24:13
content_html: The proverb shows that David was in the right and that Saul was in the wrong. David refused to do evil, even when he had the chance to kill Saul. Saul, however, repeatedly tried to kill David.

title: 1 Samuel 24:20
content_html: I know for sure that you will be king: This was Saul's first open admission of the truth (compare 1 Samuel 23:17).

title: 1 Samuel 24:21–22
content_html: swear to me … you will not cut off my descendants: Having descendants was a way that Saul’s name would endure among the living. David had already sworn such an oath with Jonathan (1 Samuel 20:14–17).

title: 1 Samuel 25:1
content_html: Paran: According to the Masoretic Text, David went to the wilderness of Paran after Samuel’s death. The Septuagint instead reads Maon, which fits better with the following story set in the Maon/Carmel area of Judah. This town in southern Judah (see study note on 1 Samuel 23:24) was probably Nabal’s home. The wilderness of Paran itself lay much farther south, in the northern Sinai near Kadesh-barnea, and is elsewhere described as a place of refuge (Genesis 21:21; 1 Kings 11:18).

title: 1 Samuel 25:1–44
content_html: This story about Nabal falls between two accounts in which Saul pursued David and David spared Saul’s life. Nabal was similar to Saul.

title: 1 Samuel 25:2
content_html: Carmel was a village near Maon. It should not be confused with the famous mountain from the story of Elijah in 1 Kings 18. In this village, Saul built a monument to celebrate his victory over the Amalekites (1 Samuel 15:12).

title: 1 Samuel 25:3
content_html:
• Nabal: See 1 Samuel 25:25 to understand the irony of his name.

  • Unlike her husband, Abigail was a sensible and wise person, as the story shows.

title: 1 Samuel 25:8
content_html: Sheep-shearing time for herdsmen was like harvest time for farmers. It was a time of celebration and thankfulness for blessings.

title: 1 Samuel 25:10
content_html: son of Jesse: This phrase refers to David. Like Saul, Nabal used this description in a disrespectful way. He avoided using David’s name to show contempt rather than honor (1 Samuel 20:27, 31; 22:7).

title: 1 Samuel 25:21
content_html: he has paid me back evil for good: See also 1 Samuel 24:17.

title: 1 Samuel 25:26
content_html: the LORD has held you back from bloodshed and from avenging yourself: Abigail began her plea by saying that God had kept David from murder and revenge, and she ended it the same way (1 Samuel 25:31). She reminded David that he had the power to kill Nabal, just as he had the power to kill Saul, but he chose not to do so.

title: 1 Samuel 25:28
content_html:
• a lasting dynasty: God promised David that his family line would continue (see 2 Samuel 7).

  • fights the LORD’s battles: David was famous and admired for defeating Israel’s enemies (1 Samuel 18:7, 13, 16).

  • no evil be found in you: This does not mean that David was without sin. It means that he was innocent in the way he pursued the throne. He did not try to seize it by violence, as 1 Samuel 25:29–31 makes clear.

title: 1 Samuel 25:29
content_html: The phrase like stones from a sling fits David well. David famously defeated Goliath by striking him with a stone from a sling (1 Samuel 17:32–51). The image reminds listeners of that event and highlights David’s strength and skill.

title: 1 Samuel 25:36
content_html: for a king: Nabal was like Saul (see study note on 1 Samuel 25:1–44).

title: 1 Samuel 25:37
content_html: Nabal was so stunned by the revelation that he had a stroke or a heart attack.

title: 1 Samuel 25:39
content_html:
• Nabal’s death was God’s punishment for his sin. David could not be accused of vengeful murder.

  • David quickly asked Abigail to marry him. She had proven herself wise and was also beautiful (1 Samuel 25:3).

title: 1 Samuel 25:43
content_html:
• Ahinoam was also the name of Saul’s wife (1 Samuel 14:50). If she was the same woman, then David married Saul’s widow after Saul died (1 Samuel 31:1–13). This would create another parallel between Nabal and Saul. However, other passages suggest they were two different women (1 Samuel 27:3; 30:5).

  • This Jezreel was a small village in Judah, near Maon, Ziph, and Carmel (Joshua 15:55–56). It was not the better-known northern city of Jezreel.

title: 1 Samuel 25:44
content_html: Saul likely gave Michal to another man for political reasons. He wanted to weaken David's claim to the throne by ending David's marriage to a royal family member.

title: 1 Samuel 26:1
content_html:
• The men from Ziph had earlier offered to capture David and give him to Saul (1 Samuel 23:19–20).

  • Hakilah opposite Jeshimon: The same place they had told Saul about before (1 Samuel 23:19). David likely stayed nearby because the hilly area had many caves, which were good for hiding.

title: 1 Samuel 26:1–25
content_html: This was the last time Saul and David were together. The Ziphites informed Saul again about David's hiding place (see 1 Samuel 23:19–20). David spared Saul's life again, just like he did in the cave at En-gedi (see 1 Samuel 24).

title: 1 Samuel 26:3
content_html: Saul went after David again, even though he had earlier admitted that he was wrong (1 Samuel 24:17–21). By this time, Saul was no longer able to think clearly or speak truthfully.

title: 1 Samuel 26:5
content_html: Abner the son of Ner: See study note on 1 Samuel 20:2.

title: 1 Samuel 26:6
content_html:
• This is the only reference to Ahimelech the Hittite.

  • Zeruiah was David’s sister (1 Chronicles 2:16), making Abishai David’s nephew. Zeruiah's sons (Abishai, Joab, and Asahel) were three of David’s most loyal followers (for example, 2 Samuel 2:18). But they sometimes caused him trouble (see 2 Samuel 3:39; 16:10; 19:22).

title: 1 Samuel 26:8
content_html: God has delivered your enemy into your hand: Compare 1 Samuel 24:4.

title: 1 Samuel 26:9
content_html: the LORD’s anointed: See study note on 1 Samuel 24:6.

title: 1 Samuel 26:10
content_html: David was confident that God would judge between him and Saul just as he had done between him and Nabal (see 1 Samuel 25:38). Saul died in battle against the Philistines (1 Samuel 31:6).

title: 1 Samuel 26:14
content_html: Abner: See study note on 1 Samuel 20:25.

title: 1 Samuel 26:16
content_html: deserve to die: Death was the punishment for failing to do one's duty.

title: 1 Samuel 26:17
content_html: Is that your voice, David my son?: See 1 Samuel 24:16.

title: 1 Samuel 26:19
content_html:
• If the LORD has stirred you up against me: David was confused by Saul’s actions and worried he might have sinned without knowing.

  • if men have done it: This might refer to Doeg (1 Samuel 22:9–10).

  • serve other gods: This idea is similar to Psalm 137:4. People exiled from the holy land could not worship the Lord through sacrifices at the main sanctuary.

title: 1 Samuel 26:20
content_html: a flea: Compare 1 Samuel 24:14.

title: 1 Samuel 26:21
content_html:
• I have sinned: Saul had realized his mistakes before (1 Samuel 24:16–19). David had no reason to trust him again (see 1 Samuel 27:1).

  • I have played the fool: The Hebrew word used here (sakal) means “to act foolishly” or “to play the fool” (see also 1 Samuel 13:13). This word is different from the word for “fool” (nabal) used in 1 Samuel 25. Even though the words are different, they have similar meanings. This helps show that Saul and Nabal are parallel figures. Both act foolishly and oppose God’s purposes.

title: 1 Samuel 26:23
content_html: David did not harm Saul (1 Samuel 24:6; 26:9) because Saul was the Lord's anointed one (see study note on 1 Samuel 24:6).

title: 1 Samuel 26:24
content_html: As surely as I valued your life today, so may the LORD value my life: David could not rely on Saul to do the same, so he trusted God with his fate.

title: 1 Samuel 26:25
content_html: Saul returned home, and this may have been David’s last personal encounter with him.

title: 1 Samuel 27:1–12
content_html: David fled to the Philistines to get away from Saul for good. Rather than killing Saul, David chose the dangerous life of a fugitive.

title: 1 Samuel 27:2
content_html: This was David’s second stay with Achish, the Philistine king (see 1 Samuel 21:10–15). On both occasions, David lied to him (see 1 Samuel 27:10).

title: 1 Samuel 27:6
content_html:
• Ziklag was about 35.4 kilometers (22 miles) southwest of Gath.

  • still belongs to the kings of Judah: This note was written after Solomon’s death in 931 BC and before the beginning of the Babylonian exile in 586 BC.

title: 1 Samuel 27:8
content_html:
• the Geshurites, the Girzites, and the Amalekites: These groups lived on the edges of the southern desert and caused problems for both the Philistines and Israelites. (For more on the Amalekites, see 1 Samuel 14:48; 15; for more on the Geshurites, see Joshua 13:2, 13; 2 Samuel 3:3.)

  • Shur was somewhere east of Egypt (1 Samuel 15:7; Genesis 16:7; 20:1; 25:18).

title: 1 Samuel 27:9
content_html: he did not leave a man or woman alive: At least some of these people were among those whom God had commanded Israel to destroy (see 1 Samuel 15:3; 28:18; Deuteronomy 25:17–19; Joshua 13:13).

title: 1 Samuel 27:10
content_html:
• David misled Achish by claiming his attacks were against his own people of Judah and two other related groups. The text neither praises nor criticizes David for lying to the foreign king.

  • The Jerahmeelites were a Semitic tribe (an ancient people group from the Middle East who shared related languages and customs) living in southern Judah (see 1 Chronicles 2:9, 25–27, 33, 42).

  • Kenites: The father-in-law of Moses was a Kenite (see Judges 1:16; 4:11; see also study note on 1 Samuel 15:6).

title: 1 Samuel 28:1
content_html: for warfare: Fights between Israel and the Philistines happened often. In exchange for providing refuge, Achish expected David’s military involvement and cooperation.

title: 1 Samuel 28:3
content_html: had removed … from the land: Saul had either forced most mediums (people who claimed they could communicate with the spirits of the dead) to leave the land or put them to death. Or he had made necromancy (the practice of trying to speak with the dead) illegal (see study note on 1 Samuel 28:9).

title: 1 Samuel 28:3–14
content_html: Saul consulted the dead, a practice forbidden by God’s law (see Leviticus 20:27; Deuteronomy 18:9–11).

title: 1 Samuel 28:4
content_html:
• Shunem was a border town in the land of Issachar (Joshua 19:18). It overlooked the Valley of Jezreel.

  • Mount Gilboa is at the east end of the Valley of Jezreel. The mountain got its name from a nearby village.

title: 1 Samuel 28:6
content_html: did not answer him: Earlier, Saul had asked the Lord for guidance using sacred lots, but the results were unclear (1 Samuel 14:36–42). God's silence was a sign of his judgment (1 Samuel 14:37; Lamentations 2:9; Ezekiel 7:26; Amos 8:11–12; Micah 3:6–7).

title: 1 Samuel 28:7
content_html:
• Without God's guidance, Saul turned to forbidden ways to learn God's will. Saul had earlier banned the occult practice that he now used himself (1 Samuel 28:3).

  • Endor was on the border between Israel and Philistia. The area near Israel’s border was probably a safer location for a banned practice.

title: 1 Samuel 28:9
content_html: killed: Literally cut off, suggesting that Saul might have had most of the mediums executed.

title: 1 Samuel 28:12
content_html: We do not know why seeing Samuel caused this woman to recognize Saul.

title: 1 Samuel 28:13
content_html: Saul asked the medium to describe what she saw that caused her to scream. She described a divine being, a god (Hebrew ’elohim) coming up. erhaps she observed a human figure with a godlike or angelic appearance, or her use of the term god might reflect a pagan belief that a person becomes a god (a spirit possessing supernatural abilities) after death.

title: 1 Samuel 28:15
content_html:
• God has turned away from me: The same Hebrew term appears in 1 Samuel 16:14 and Judges 16:20.

  • no longer answers me, either by prophets or by dreams: Compare 1 Samuel 28:6.

title: 1 Samuel 28:16
content_html: Saul’s enemy was not David but the Lord himself.

title: 1 Samuel 28:17
content_html: torn the kingship out of your hand: See 1 Samuel 15:28.

title: 1 Samuel 28:18
content_html: you did not obey: Samuel's message to Saul had not changed (compare 1 Samuel 15:19).

title: 1 Samuel 28:19
content_html: will be with me: In Sheol, the place where the dead are believed to dwell (compare 1 Samuel 2:6, to Sheol; see also Numbers 16:30–33; Job 17:1–16; Psalm 49:11; Revelation 1:18).

title: 1 Samuel 28:24
content_html: fattened calf … flour … and baked unleavened bread: Preparing this meal required much time and effort. It showed generous hospitality (compare Genesis 18:5–7). The meal that the medium shared with Saul recalls the fine meal that Samuel once shared with Saul (1 Samuel 9:22–24). That earlier meal, shared with God’s prophet, marked the beginning of Saul’s kingship. This later meal, shared with a condemned medium, came shortly before Saul’s death.

title: 1 Samuel 29:1
content_html:
• Aphek was the site of an earlier confrontation between Israel and the Philistines (1 Samuel 4:1).

  • Jezreel was about 64.4 kilometers (40 miles) northeast of Aphek.

title: 1 Samuel 29:1–11
content_html: God's help saved David from having to fight his own people.

title: 1 Samuel 29:3
content_html:
• the servant of King Saul of Israel: To Achish, David appeared to be a valuable defector (someone who had left his former king). The other Philistine rulers were suspicious. They feared that David was a spy who planned to attack them from inside their defenses.

  • found no fault in him: This was the first of three times Achish defended David (see also 1 Samuel 29:6, 9). However, David had been deceiving Achish the entire time (see 1 Samuel 27:1–12).

title: 1 Samuel 29:4
content_html: with the heads of our men: The commanders' fears were justified (compare 1 Samuel 17:51; 18:27).

title: 1 Samuel 29:5
content_html: This song referred to Philistines whom David had killed (1 Samuel 18:7). Because of this, it is surprising that David was able to find safety among them.

title: 1 Samuel 29:6
content_html:
• as the LORD: Achish used the unique name of Israel’s God to prove his own sincerity.

  • no fault in you: This statement was not completely true, but Achish did not realize this (1 Samuel 27:8–12).

title: 1 Samuel 30:1
content_html:
• Ziklag: See study note on 1 Samuel 27:6.

  • The Amalekites might have been striking back because David raided them (1 Samuel 27:8).

title: 1 Samuel 30:2
content_html:
• There were no soldiers left in Ziklag to protect the women and children. The Amalekites were known for attacking the weak and vulnerable (see Deuteronomy 25:17–18).

  • not killed anyone: The Amalekites were not being kind. They found women and children more valuable alive than dead.

title: 1 Samuel 30:5
content_html:
• Ahinoam: See the study note on 1 Samuel 25:43.

  • Abigail: See 1 Samuel 25:3–42.

title: 1 Samuel 30:6
content_html: greatly distressed: Saul had used the same expression about himself. When he was in deep trouble, Saul turned to necromancy (trying to speak with the dead). This led to disaster (1 Samuel 28:15). David, in contrast, found strength and success in the Lord his God.

title: 1 Samuel 30:7
content_html: the ephod: See study notes on 1 Samuel 2:18 and 23:6.

title: 1 Samuel 30:8
content_html: Unlike Saul (1 Samuel 28:6), David successfully asked the Lord for guidance (see 1 Samuel 23:9–12; 2 Samuel 2:1; 5:19, 23).

title: 1 Samuel 30:9
content_html: Besor was a large stream in Philistine territory. It flowed into the Mediterranean Sea southwest of Gaza.

title: 1 Samuel 30:10
content_html: too exhausted: David's men were very tired after traveling for three days (1 Samuel 30:1). They were now chasing the Amalekites without having rested. David did not scold them or force them to keep going without mercy. See 1 Samuel 30:23–25.

title: 1 Samuel 30:11–12
content_html: David once received bread when he was very hungry (1 Samuel 21:3–7). He and his men helped this Egyptian man before knowing who he was or if he could assist them.

title: 1 Samuel 30:13
content_html: when I fell ill: The Amalekites valued only slaves who were healthy and strong. They considered the others useless and left them behind.

title: 1 Samuel 30:14
content_html: The land of Caleb was in the wilderness of Judah, south of Hebron (see Joshua 14:6–15).

title: 1 Samuel 30:17
content_html: The total number of Amalekite raiders must have been quite large if 400 young men escaped.

title: 1 Samuel 30:21
content_html: he greeted them: David did not scold them for not participating in the battle.

title: 1 Samuel 30:21–31
content_html: David generously divided the spoils among those who participated in the battle and those who were too exhausted to take part.

title: 1 Samuel 30:22
content_html: They may take them and go: This unkind group wanted to send away those who had not taken part, without giving them any reward.

title: 1 Samuel 30:23
content_html: what the LORD has given us: Some of David's men believed their success came from their own military skills. David attributed victory to God and gave him the glory.

title: 1 Samuel 30:24
content_html: They will share alike: Compare with Numbers 31:25–47, where Moses divided the plunder of war between those who fought and the rest of the people.

title: 1 Samuel 30:26
content_html: his friends, the elders of Judah: David extended his generosity to influential people throughout his own tribal area, possibly to secure their support for him as king.

title: 1 Samuel 30:27–30
content_html: The places mentioned here were all in southern Judah. David eventually emerged from this territory as king (see 2 Samuel 2:1–4).

title: 1 Samuel 30:31
content_html: David ruled as king over Judah from the city of Hebron for seven and a half years (2 Samuel 2:1–7; 5:5).

title: 1 Samuel 31:1
content_html:
• The Israelites fled southeast from the Valley of Jezreel (1 Samuel 29:1) to higher and hillier areas. With their chariots, the Philistines had the advantage in the more level valley.

  • Mount Gilboa is southwest of the Sea of Galilee, far from the territory of the Philistines.

title: 1 Samuel 31:1–13
content_html: While David was in the south successfully fighting the Amalekites (1 Samuel 30), Saul was in the north unsuccessfully fighting the Philistines.

title: 1 Samuel 31:2
content_html: Jonathan … Malkishua: See 1 Samuel 14:49. This is the first mention of Saul’s son Abinadab (compare 1 Chronicles 8:33; 9:39).

title: 1 Samuel 31:4
content_html:
• David had earlier served as an armor bearer (a personal assistant who carried a soldier’s weapons) to Saul (1 Samuel 16:21).

  • run me through: This is one of many similarities between the death of Abimelech during the time of the judges (Judges 9) and Saul, Israel’s first national king (see Judges 9:54). In both stories, a wounded leader asks someone else to kill him rather than die in shame.

title: 1 Samuel 31:8
content_html: After a victory, the army would remove items from the dead. They searched the bodies for anything valuable, such as clothing, weapons, or jewelry.

title: 1 Samuel 31:9
content_html: In the ancient world, people saw the death of an enemy king as good news. They believed it meant that the enemy’s god had been defeated. The Philistines did not realize that their victory would not last and would bring them no real gain. David, the Lord’s true anointed king, would soon come to power and defeat the Philistines. Instead of defeating Israel’s God, the Philistines were unknowingly carrying out his purpose.

title: 1 Samuel 31:10
content_html:
• the Ashtoreths: See 1 Samuel 7:3.

  • hung his body: This act further humiliated the executed person by denying them a proper burial. It also served as a warning to other potential enemies (see also Genesis 40:19; Deuteronomy 21:21–22; Joshua 10:26; Esther 9:6–14).

  • Beth-shan was a short distance east of Mount Gilboa.

title: 1 Samuel 31:11
content_html: The people of Jabesh-gilead were repaying a kindness. Earlier, Saul had rescued them from the Ammonites and their king Nahash (1 Samuel 11:1–13). Jabesh-gilead was about nineteen kilometers (twelve miles) southeast of Beth-shan. It lay across the Jordan River.

title: 1 Samuel 31:12
content_html: burned the bodies: Cremation was uncommon in the ancient world. The bodies may have been burned because decomposition had already begun.

title: 1 Samuel 31:13
content_html:
• bones … Jabesh: This was not where Saul's remains were finally laid to rest (see 2 Samuel 21:12–14).

  • The end of 1 Samuel returns Israel's history to the time at the end of Judges, when “Israel had no king.” The Philistines were in control again. David had God’s anointing but no crown. He had wives but no sons. His connection with the hated Philistines may have put his standing with his own people at risk. David's future would be difficult. Still, it was clear that God was with him.
